title: Synthetic Identity Bust-Out: Staged Credit Building to Sudden Max-Out
narrative: A synthetic identity establishes credibility through 3-6 months of controlled small purchases ($15-$85 range) at legitimate merchants, building card1_txn_count_7d gradually from 2-4 transactions weekly. Account maintains clean payment history with P_emaildomain stable (gmail.com, yahoo.com) and consistent addr1. Between days 90-180, behavior shifts dramatically: 8-12 high-value transactions ($800-$2,500 each) execute within 48-72 hours, targeting electronics and gift card MCCs. TransactionAmt jumps create card1_amt_zscore exceeding 4.5 compared to prior history. Bust-out phase shows R_emaildomain suddenly differing from P_emaildomain, dist1 increases to 500+ miles from established patterns, and txn_hour clusters in is_night windows (2-5 AM) versus prior daytime activity. No attempt at repayment follows maxing total credit lines.
indicators: card1_txn_count_180d shows 35-60 small transactions ($15-$85) then 8-12 large transactions ($800-$2,500) in final 48-72 hours
card1_amt_zscore spikes from baseline 0.3-0.8 to 4.5+ during final transaction cluster
R_emaildomain changes or appears for first time during bust-out phase while P_emaildomain remains stable
dist1 increases from consistent <50 miles to 500+ miles for high-value transactions
txn_hour pattern shifts from 70%+ daytime (9-20 hours) to 60%+ is_night (2-5 AM) during maxing phase
ProductCD concentration in final 48 hours: 6+ transactions to electronics/gift card categories versus prior retail diversity
Time between account opening and bust-out: 90-180 days with clean payment history then sudden default
summary: Account shows 3-6 months of small, regular purchases ($15-$85) establishing normal patterns, then suddenly executes 8-12 high-value transactions ($800-$2,500) within 48-72 hours, often at night and from new locations 500+ miles away. The transaction amount spikes to 4.5+ standard deviations above historical baseline, email domains change, and merchant types shift to electronics and gift cards.
reasoning: Fraudsters create fake identities and nurture them with months of controlled activity to build credit limits and bypass fraud filters, then execute a coordinated bust-out by maxing all available credit with no intention of repayment. The dramatic shift in spending patterns, timing, location, and merchant categories reveals the account was never a real customer but a staged vehicle for theft.
indicator_explanations: Account shows months of small legitimate purchases then suddenly maxes out in days
Transaction amounts explode to multiples of the card's historical spending pattern
Recipient email switches right when the bust-out starts but sender stays the same
High-value purchases suddenly happen hundreds of miles from the established usage area
Shopping shifts from normal business hours to middle-of-the-night during the attack
Final spree concentrates on easily resold items like electronics and gift cards
Account ages cleanly for months then abruptly defaults after maxing out credit
